evidence: BlackLabSnippetParser.detokenize() returns null only when its argument is null. In trimToSentence(), `assembled` is built from string concatenation (never null), so `detokenize(assembled)` never returns null; the fallback branch `detokenized != null ? detokenized : assembled` at line 88 is dead code.

Same pattern at line 130 in extractPlainTextFromXml(): `text` is the result of chained .replaceAll().replaceAll().trim() on a guarded non-null string, so the fallback `detokenized != null ? detokenized : text` is never taken.

Main.java uses `System.exit(1); throw new AssertionError("unreachable");` in parseIntParam and parseDoubleParam — the AssertionError throw is dead code, but it is a documented compiler-satisfaction idiom and labelled 'unreachable', so it is intentional.
HttpApiUtils.parseQueryParams catches UnsupportedEncodingException with a comment 'UTF-8 is always supported; this branch is unreachable in practice' — intentional dead code with explanatory comment.

Guard clauses throughout the codebase (null/empty checks before processing) are used consistently and correctly in BlackLabQueryExecutor, CollocateQueryHelper, BlackLabSnippetParser, and the exploration layer.
No identical if/else branches found. Boolean logic is direct throughout; no `if x: return true else: return false` anti-patterns observed.
Control flow in BlackLabQueryExecutor.executeCqlQuery, executeDependencyPattern, and executeCollocations is straightforward with clean early-return paths when lemma or deprel is absent.
VisualizationHandlers switch over RenderMode uses null-case and SIGNED explicitly, which is exhaustive and correct for the current single-enum-value definition.

strengths: Guard clauses used consistently across all query executor and exploration methods — null/empty checks placed at the entry point of every public method before any logic runs.
Control flow in the collocate-ranking pipeline (CollocateQueryHelper) is linear and explicit: collect hits, pre-fetch frequencies, score, sort, limit — no hidden branching or unclear short-circuits.
No identical if/else branches or always-true/false conditions observed in any of the 23 reviewed files.
Boolean expressions are direct and unambiguous throughout; every conditional produces or guards a meaningful side effect rather than wrapping a trivial boolean return.
logic_clarity.issue_character: The issues are isolated to a single method (detokenize) and its two call sites in BlackLabSnippetParser: the @Nullable return annotation creates an unreachable null-fallback branch that is dead code in both callers, because both callers always pass a non-null string.
logic_clarity.score_rationale: The codebase is largely solid for logic_clarity: guards are correct and consistent, control flow across the 23 reviewed files is predictable, and no structural confusers (identical branches, always-true conditions, async-wrapping-sync) were found. One real finding pulls the score down from the high-80s: the redundant dead-fallback in detokenize callers indicates that the @Nullable contract of detokenize is misleading at its call sites, creating silent dead code in two places. The intentional unreachable patterns (AssertionError after System.exit, unreachable UTF-8 catch) are documented and should not be penalised. Placing this at 80 — solid but with a small, concrete logic clarity gap in one utility class.

issues.summary: Null-fallback after detokenize() is dead code at both call sites
evidence: detokenize(String text) returns null only when its argument is null (line 140: `if (text == null || text.isEmpty()) return text;` — the null return path requires null input).

In trimToSentence() at line 88: `assembled` is produced by string concatenation of non-null prefix/suffix literals and `match`; even if `match` is null, Java string concat yields the literal string 'null', so `assembled` is never null. `detokenize(assembled)` therefore never returns null, and the fallback `? ... : assembled` is never executed.

In extractPlainTextFromXml() at line 130: `text` is the result of `.replaceAll(...).replaceAll(...).trim()` on a non-null `xmlSnippet` (already guarded at line 125). `detokenize(text)` never returns null, so the fallback `? ... : text` is never executed.
The @Nullable annotation on detokenize's return type correctly documents the null-input contract, but since no caller ever passes null, both null-checks are unreachable.
issues.suggestion: Either change detokenize to @NonNull (removing the null-input path and replacing `return text` with `return ""`), or change the two callers to use the result directly without the ternary: `return detokenize(assembled);` and `return detokenize(text);`. The simpler fix is to remove the null-input case from detokenize since all callers guard against null before calling it.

root_causes: The @Nullable return annotation on detokenize was added defensively but the method is only called after null-guards, so the null-return branch and its downstream ternary fallbacks became dead code without a compiler warning.
